title: The Six Guarantees of the Congress Government
intro: When the Congress government led by CM A. Revanth Reddy took office in Telangana in December 2023, it promised 'Six Guarantees'. These are the backbone of every scheme question in TGPSC now.
explanation:
- **Mahalakshmi** — support for women: free bus travel, LPG cylinder at ₹500, and financial assistance to women.
- **Rythu Bharosa** — investment support for farmers and farm labourers, plus crop loan waiver.
- **Gruha Jyothi** — free electricity up to **200 units** per month for eligible households.
- **Indiramma Indlu** — housing for the poor and house-site support.
- **Yuva Vikasam** — support for students' education (Vidya Bharosa).
- **Cheyutha** — enhanced pensions for the elderly, widows, and persons with disabilities.
The government began rolling these out from **December 2023** onwards, starting with free bus travel for women.
example: In the very first cabinet decisions after swearing-in (December 2023), the government launched **free travel for women in TGSRTC ordinary buses** (Mahalakshmi) — an example of a guarantee converted into a scheme within days.

test.q: How many 'guarantees' did the Congress government in Telangana promise in 2023?
test.why: The 'Six Guarantees' — Mahalakshmi, Rythu Bharosa, Gruha Jyothi, Indiramma Indlu, Yuva Vikasam and Cheyutha.

test.q: Who became Chief Minister of Telangana in December 2023?
test.why: A. Revanth Reddy led the Congress to power and took oath on 7 December 2023.

test.q: Gruha Jyothi provides free electricity up to:
test.why: Eligible households get up to 200 units free per month.

test.q: Which guarantee is aimed at housing for the poor?
test.why: Indiramma Indlu is the housing guarantee.

test.q: Which scheme was implemented first, within days of the new government taking office?
test.why: Free travel for women in ordinary TGSRTC buses started on 9 December 2023.

title: Mahalakshmi Scheme — Where It Applies and Who Benefits
intro: Let's learn about the Mahalakshmi scheme — the flagship women-welfare guarantee — where it is applicable and how many people use it.
explanation:
- **Free bus travel**: women and transgender persons can travel **free in TGSRTC ordinary buses (Palle Velugu and Express) within Telangana** — not in AC/luxury buses and not outside the state. Passengers get **'zero tickets'** so the government can reimburse TGSRTC.
- **Scale**: crores of zero tickets are issued every month; government figures have cited **hundreds of crores of free journeys** since launch, making it one of India's largest free-transport schemes for women.
- **₹500 LPG cylinder**: subsidised cooking-gas refills for eligible (white ration card) households under Mahalakshmi.
- **Objective**: increase women's mobility, workforce participation and savings for poor families.
- **Exam angle**: remember launch date (9 December 2023), the operator (TGSRTC), and that it applies only to **ordinary buses within the state**.
example: A woman travelling from Warangal to Hyderabad in a Palle Velugu or Express bus shows her ID, receives a zero ticket and pays nothing — but the same journey in a Rajdhani AC bus is charged, because the scheme covers only ordinary services.

test.q: Mahalakshmi free bus travel applies to:
test.why: Only Palle Velugu/Express (ordinary) TGSRTC services within the state are covered.

test.q: The 'zero ticket' issued under the scheme is used to:
test.why: Zero tickets record free journeys so the government can compensate the corporation.

test.q: Under Mahalakshmi, an LPG cylinder is supplied to eligible households at:
test.why: The guarantee provides gas refills at ₹500 for eligible families.

test.q: Free bus travel under Mahalakshmi began on:
test.why: It was launched on 9 December 2023, two days after the government took oath.

test.q: Besides women, who else is eligible for free bus travel?
test.why: The scheme covers women and transgender persons resident in Telangana.

title: Rythu Bharosa and the ₹2 Lakh Crop Loan Waiver
intro: Let's learn about the farmer-support guarantee — how it changed from Rythu Bandhu, and the massive 2024 loan waiver.
explanation:
- **Rythu Bharosa** replaced the earlier BRS government's *Rythu Bandhu*: investment support to farmers raised to **₹12,000 per acre per year** (from ₹10,000), credited in two crop-season instalments. Rollout began around **Sankranti 2025**.
- **Indiramma Atmiya Bharosa**: a new companion scheme giving **₹12,000 per year to landless agricultural labourer families** — a group Rythu Bandhu had left out.
- **Crop loan waiver**: in **2024** the government waived crop loans up to **₹2 lakh per farmer family**, released in tranches (up to ₹1 lakh, then ₹1.5 lakh, then ₹2 lakh) during July–August 2024, benefiting lakhs of farmers.
- **Exam angle**: compare Rythu Bandhu (per-acre, landowners only) with Rythu Bharosa (higher amount + labourers covered via Atmiya Bharosa + talk of excluding non-cultivable land).
example: A tenant-village labourer family that owned no land got nothing under Rythu Bandhu, but under **Indiramma Atmiya Bharosa** it receives ₹12,000 a year — an example of widening a scheme's coverage to the truly landless.

test.q: Under Rythu Bharosa, farmers receive investment support of:
test.why: Support was raised to ₹12,000 per acre per year from Rythu Bandhu's ₹10,000.

test.q: The 2024 Telangana crop loan waiver covered loans up to:
test.why: Crop loans up to ₹2 lakh per farmer family were waived in tranches in 2024.

test.q: Indiramma Atmiya Bharosa gives ₹12,000 a year to:
test.why: It extends income support to landless farm-labour families.

test.q: Rythu Bharosa replaced which earlier scheme?
test.why: It is the Congress government's successor to the BRS-era Rythu Bandhu.

test.q: The Rythu Bharosa rollout is associated with which festival season of 2025?
test.why: Disbursal began around Sankranti (January) 2025.

title: Gruha Jyothi and Indiramma Indlu
intro: Two guarantees touch every poor household directly — free power (Gruha Jyothi) and housing (Indiramma Indlu). Let's learn how they work.
explanation:
- **Gruha Jyothi** (launched **March 2024**): eligible households (white ration card) consuming up to **200 units a month** pay **zero** electricity bill. If consumption crosses 200 units, the **full bill** must be paid — the limit is a cliff, not a discount.
- **Indiramma Indlu** (launched 2024–25): financial assistance of **₹5 lakh** for construction of a house for the homeless poor; those without land are to get a **house site plus construction support**. Beneficiary selection is done through Gram Sabhas and an app-based survey; the scheme honours the legacy name 'Indiramma' (Indira Gandhi) used in earlier housing programmes.
- **Exam angle**: know the unit ceiling (200), assistance amount (₹5 lakh), launch years, and that both target ration-card holding poor families.
example: A family that uses 180 units in a month pays nothing under Gruha Jyothi; a family using 210 units pays the entire bill. This 'threshold' design is often asked as a tricky MCQ.

test.q: Under Gruha Jyothi, a household using 210 units in a month pays:
test.why: Crossing the 200-unit ceiling means the entire bill is payable.

test.q: Indiramma Indlu provides house-construction assistance of:
test.why: ₹5 lakh is provided for building a house; landless beneficiaries also get a site.

test.q: Gruha Jyothi was launched in:
test.why: Free power up to 200 units began from March 2024.

test.q: The name 'Indiramma' in the housing scheme refers to:
test.why: The scheme name continues the legacy of Indira Gandhi-named housing programmes.

test.q: Eligibility for Gruha Jyothi is mainly linked to:
test.why: The benefit targets poor households identified through ration cards.

title: Caste Survey (SEEEPC) and the 42% BC Reservation Move
intro: One of the most important incidents of 2024–25: Telangana counted the social composition of its entire population — the first big caste survey by a state in decades.
explanation:
- The **Socio, Economic, Educational, Employment, Political and Caste (SEEEPC) Survey** was conducted door-to-door from **November 2024** and its findings were presented in early **2025**.
- Key finding: **Backward Classes form a majority** of the state's population (around 56% including BC Muslims), strengthening the demand for higher BC quotas.
- Following the survey, the Assembly passed **bills to raise BC reservations to 42%** in education, employment and **local body elections**; since the total would cross the 50% ceiling (Indra Sawhney case), the bills were sent for the President's/central consideration and reservation in local bodies was pushed through ordinance/GO routes — a live legal-political issue.
- **Exam angle**: name of survey, month/year, headline percentages, the 50% ceiling problem, and the connection to the promised 42% BC quota.
example: Panchayat elections became the test case: the government sought to apply **42% BC reservation in local bodies** based on the survey's data — showing how a statistical exercise directly drives policy and litigation.

test.q: The Telangana caste survey of 2024–25 is officially called:
test.why: It is the Socio, Economic, Educational, Employment, Political and Caste survey.

test.q: The survey found which group forms a majority of Telangana's population?
test.why: BCs (including BC Muslims) were found to be around 56% of the population.

test.q: The government moved to raise BC reservation to what level?
test.why: Bills were passed for 42% BC quota in education, jobs and local bodies.

test.q: The 50% ceiling on reservations comes from which Supreme Court case?
test.why: Indra Sawhney (1992) capped total reservation at 50% barring exceptional cases.

test.q: The door-to-door caste survey began in:
test.why: Enumeration started in November 2024 and findings came in early 2025.

title: SIR Form — Special Intensive Revision of Electoral Rolls
intro: An important current-affairs update: the Election Commission of India's Special Intensive Revision (SIR) of electoral rolls, and the enumeration form every voter must know about.
explanation:
- **SIR (Special Intensive Revision)** is an intensive, house-to-house **re-verification of the entire electoral roll** by the Election Commission of India — much deeper than the routine annual 'summary revision'.
- It began with **Bihar in 2025** and was extended in phases to more states/UTs, with Telangana in the later phases.
- **The SIR form (Enumeration Form)**: Booth Level Officers (BLOs) distribute pre-printed forms to every existing elector; the voter fills in details (and, where required, documents establishing eligibility/citizenship) and returns it. Names of those who don't return forms risk moving to the **draft-exclusion list**, with a claims-and-objections window to get re-included.
- **Why it matters**: it decides who can vote, sparked debates about migrant workers and documentation, and produced court cases — a hot topic for both Polity and Current Affairs sections.
example: A daily-wage migrant from Telangana working in Mumbai must ensure their SIR enumeration form is submitted back home (family or BLO help), otherwise their name may not appear in the draft roll and they must file a claim during the objections period to be re-added.

test.q: SIR in the electoral context stands for:
test.why: SIR = Special Intensive Revision of electoral rolls by the ECI.

test.q: SIR is conducted by:
test.why: Preparing and revising electoral rolls is the ECI's constitutional function (Article 324).

test.q: The 2025 SIR exercise began first in which state?
test.why: Bihar's rolls were revised first, ahead of its assembly elections.

test.q: Enumeration forms under SIR are distributed door-to-door by:
test.why: BLOs hand out and collect the enumeration forms.

test.q: A voter who fails to return the SIR form risks:
options: Exclusion from the draft electoral roll
test.why: Non-returned forms can lead to exclusion from the draft roll, subject to claims and objections.

title: Cheyutha, Yuva Vikasam and Other Welfare Measures
intro: Let's complete the guarantee picture: pensions (Cheyutha), student support (Yuva Vikasam), and other headline welfare promises.
explanation:
- **Cheyutha**: promise of enhanced monthly pensions — **₹4,000** for the elderly, widows, single women, beedi workers etc., and **₹6,000** for persons with disabilities (continuing and enhancing the earlier Aasara pensions).
- **Yuva Vikasam**: **Vidya Bharosa cards worth up to ₹5 lakh** promised for students' higher-education costs; the government has also pushed **Young India Integrated Residential Schools** and international-standard skill development (Young India Skills University, launched 2024).
- **Rajiv Aarogyasri**: health-insurance cover raised (promise of up to **₹10 lakh** per family).
- **Mahatma Jyotiba Phule Praja Bhavan**: the old Pragathi Bhavan renamed and opened for public grievance (Praja Vani/Praja Palana programmes for receiving applications).
- **Exam angle**: match each scheme to its target group; note renamings (RTC→TGSRTC, state emblem changes) as 'important incidents'.
example: Under **Praja Palana** (December 2023–January 2024), the government collected over a crore applications from citizens for the six guarantees at village-level camps — the largest application drive in the state's history and a common exam fact.

test.q: Cheyutha promises a monthly pension for persons with disabilities of:
test.why: PwDs are promised ₹6,000; most other categories ₹4,000.

test.q: Vidya Bharosa cards under Yuva Vikasam are worth up to:
test.why: The guarantee promises up to ₹5 lakh support for a student's education.

test.q: The application-collection drive for the six guarantees in Dec 2023–Jan 2024 was called:
test.why: Praja Palana camps collected guarantee applications across the state.

test.q: The skills university launched in Telangana in 2024 is named:
test.why: The Young India Skills University came up with industry partnership in 2024.

test.q: Pragathi Bhavan was renamed as:
test.why: It was renamed after Jyotiba Phule and opened for public grievance programmes.
